sections.heading: Use among currently married women stayed almost flat, but the mix moved toward modern methods
sections.body: Among currently married women, any contraceptive use was 58.6 percent in 2025, almost unchanged from 58.3 percent in 2022. The stable total masks a shift inside the mix: modern method use rose from 41.8 percent to 44.5 percent, while traditional method use fell from 16.5 percent to 14.1 percent. The pill remained the largest single method at 20.5 percent, while injectables, implants, and male condoms all increased from 2022. Compared with the all-women framing, where any-method use was 33.8 percent in 2025, this page shows the method mix among women for whom current contraceptive use is more directly concentrated.

sections.heading: The highest-use regions do not all have the same method profile
sections.body: Davao Region had the highest any-method use among currently married women at 70.4 percent, followed by Ilocos Region at 67.5 percent and Central Luzon at 65.0 percent. Davao Region's position is driven mainly by modern methods, with modern use at 58.2 percent and traditional use at 12.2 percent. Ilocos Region reached nearly the same overall level through a different mix, with modern use at 45.3 percent and the highest traditional-method use in the country at 22.3 percent. The regional story is therefore not only about how many women use contraception, but also about which method mix produces that level.

sections.heading: BARMM remains a clear outlier below the rest of the country
sections.body: BARMM was far below every other region, with any-method use at 25.3 percent among currently married women in 2025. Its modern-method use was 20.2 percent and traditional-method use was 5.1 percent, both the lowest regional values. The next-lowest regions for any-method use were Central Visayas at 47.8 percent and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ at 49.8 percent, still more than 20 percentage points above BARMM. This makes BARMM an isolated low-use outlier rather than simply the bottom end of a smooth regional gradient.

sections.heading: Traditional-method use is concentrated in a different set of regions
sections.body: Traditional-method use was highest in Ilocos Region at 22.3 percent, Eastern Visayas at 20.4 percent, and CALABARZON at 18.1 percent. These are not the same regions that lead modern-method use, where Davao Region, MIMAROPA, and Caraga were at the top. Some high-use regions therefore still rely heavily on traditional methods, while others reach high use through modern methods. That distinction matters because the same overall contraceptive-use rate can reflect very different patterns of method choice.

sections.heading: Modern-method use varies more widely by region than traditional use
sections.body: Modern-method use ranged from 58.2 percent in Davao Region to 20.2 percent in BARMM, a spread of 38.0 percentage points. Traditional-method use ranged from 22.3 percent in Ilocos Region to 5.1 percent in BARMM, a narrower spread of 17.2 percentage points. Several regions clustered near the national modern-method level of 44.5 percent, including Western Visayas at 44.9 percent, Eastern Visayas at 44.0 percent, and CALABARZON at 42.1 percent. The sharper geographic separation is in modern-method use, not in traditional-method use alone.

sections.heading: Use peaks from the late twenties through the late thirties, then drops among older married women
sections.body: Any contraceptive use was highest among currently married women aged 35 to 39 at 64.1 percent and aged 30 to 34 at 62.9 percent. Women aged 20 to 24 and 25 to 29 were close behind at 60.0 percent and 59.9 percent, so the high-use span covers much of the main childbearing years. Use was much lower among women aged 45 to 49 at 46.0 percent and among married women aged 15 to 19 at 42.6 percent. Modern methods followed a similar age pattern, reaching 48.7 percent among women aged 35 to 39 and 48.3 percent among those aged 30 to 34.

sections.heading: The number of living children shows where use is most concentrated
sections.body: Currently married women with three or four living children had the highest contraceptive use at 70.9 percent in 2025. Modern-method use was also highest in this group at 59.1 percent, well above women with one or two children at 42.1 percent and women with no children at 7.6 percent. Women with five or more children still had high overall use at 61.3 percent, but slightly lower than those with three or four children. Traditional-method use was highest among women with one or two children at 16.7 percent, showing that method mix shifts as family size changes.

sections.heading: Residence shows a small overall gap but a clearer method-mix difference
sections.body: Rural currently married women had slightly higher any-method use than urban women, 59.4 percent compared with 57.9 percent. The larger difference is in method mix: rural modern-method use was 47.2 percent, compared with 42.1 percent in urban areas. Urban women had higher traditional-method use, 15.8 percent compared with 12.2 percent in rural areas. The residence pattern is therefore less about whether women use contraception and more about whether current use is modern or traditional.

sections.heading: Education differences are not a simple step-by-step gradient
sections.body: Any-method use was highest among women with Grades 7-10 education at 61.6 percent, followed closely by Grades 11-12 at 60.2 percent and post-secondary education at 60.0 percent. College-educated women were lower at 56.0 percent, similar to women with Grades 1-6 education at 56.1 percent. Modern-method use was highest among women with Grades 7-10 education at 48.3 percent and lowest among college-educated women at 39.3 percent, aside from women with no education at 34.8 percent. Traditional-method use moved in the other direction, reaching 18.4 percent among women with post-secondary education and 16.6 percent among college-educated women.

sections.heading: Wealth differences are modest for total use, but method mix tilts sharply
sections.body: Any-method use among currently married women was fairly compressed across wealth quintiles, from 53.6 percent in the highest quintile to 61.8 percent in the second quintile. Modern-method use was highest in the lowest and second quintiles, at 48.6 percent and 48.2 percent, and lowest in the highest quintile at 37.7 percent. Traditional-method use was highest in the middle and highest quintiles, at 16.0 percent and 15.9 percent. The wealth pattern is therefore less a simple high-low difference in use than a shift from modern methods toward traditional methods as wealth rises.

sections.heading: Sexually active unmarried women show lower use but a strong shift away from traditional methods
sections.body: Among sexually active unmarried women, any contraceptive use fell from 41.4 percent in 2022 to 37.9 percent in 2025. Modern-method use still rose from 23.8 percent to 26.2 percent, while traditional-method use dropped sharply from 17.6 percent to 11.7 percent. The method details show a large rise in pill use, from 7.1 percent to 15.3 percent, alongside a decline in male condom use from 11.9 percent to 6.6 percent and withdrawal from 15.0 percent to 10.4 percent. This group should not be folded into the married-women pattern, because its overall level and method shifts move differently.

sections.heading: Among sexually active unmarried women, rural use is higher because traditional use is higher
sections.body: Sexually active unmarried women in rural areas had higher any-method use than those in urban areas, 41.0 percent compared with 36.4 percent. Modern-method use was nearly the same, at 26.7 percent in rural areas and 26.0 percent in urban areas. The residence gap comes mainly from traditional methods, which were 14.4 percent in rural areas and 10.3 percent in urban areas. That makes the rural advantage in total use less a sign of higher modern-method coverage than of a different method mix.

takeaways: Among currently married women, contraceptive use was stable from 2022 to 2025, but modern methods made up more of current use.
Regional differences are large and method-specific: Davao Region leads through modern methods, while Ilocos Region combines high total use with high traditional-method use.
Sexually active unmarried women have a separate pattern, with lower total use, rising modern use, and a sharp decline in traditional methods.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
